hints.est only: There are two finite verbs: 'est' (is) and 'fuerit' (shall have been)
hints.fuerit only: 'fuerit' is a finite verb, but so is 'est' — the main clause verb
hints.scandalizatus and est: 'scandalizatus' is a participle, not a finite verb — the verbs are 'est' and 'fuerit'

hints.to eat: 'to eat' is 'edere' — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.perfect: Perfect of 'esse' would be 'fuit' — 'est' is present

hints.edo, edere, edi, esum: These are principal parts of 'edo' (to eat)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are principal parts of 'sto' (to stand)
hints.sedeo, sedere, sedi, sessum: These are principal parts of 'sedeo' (to sit)

hints.to become: 'to become' is 'fieri' — 'fuerit' is from 'esse' (to be), perfect stem 'fu-'
hints.to flee: 'to flee' is 'fugere' — 'fuerit' is from 'esse' with the stem 'fu-'

reveal.gloss: shall have been / who has not been

hints.future perfect indicative: 'fuerit' in a relative clause with 'qui' is best read as perfect subjunctive
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect indicative 3rd person is 'fuit' — 'fuerit' is subjunctive
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'fuisset' — 'fuerit' is perfect subjunctive

hints.fio, fieri, factus sum, —: These are principal parts of 'fio' (to become/be made)
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are principal parts of 'fugio' (to flee)
hints.fundo, fundere, fudi, fusum: These are principal parts of 'fundo' (to pour)

hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'beatum' — 'Beatus' is nominative (predicate adjective)
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'beate' — 'Beatus' is nominative, predicate of 'est'

translation: Blessed is he who shall not have been scandalized
